nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs and rocky substrates at depths of approximately 2–20 m

decorPreferences: mature tank with established algae growth

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented consuming zoantharians, polychaete worms, gorgonian polyps, stony coral tissue, and tunicates. In captivity, tends to be difficult to wean onto prepared foods; vitamin-enriched live brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, finely chopped clam, squid, and frozen carnivore preparations have been reported to work for some individuals. Feeding small amounts at least three times daily is generally recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: Chris Huss / Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary; 1764x1176. Sharp pair of adults in blue water showing the white-ringed false eyespot near the tail, dark eye-bar and fine chevron pattern; a pair shot best represents this monogamous-pairing species.
Dan Schofield; 2048x1535. Crisp, vibrant single adult in open blue water, full body in frame with prominent ocellus, dark eye-bar and yellow fins clearly shown.
Dan Schofield; 2048x1537. Striking dark-background side profile highlighting the false eyespot and fine line pattern; sharp, different setting for variety.

summary: A striking Caribbean butterflyfish known for its distinctive false eyespot near the tail. Generally considered one of the more challenging marines to keep — tends to refuse prepared foods and has been reported to have high mortality in captivity.
careNotes:
The Four Eyed Butterflyfish (also sold as Foureye or Mock-eye Butterflyfish) is widely regarded as one of the more demanding Caribbean marine fish in the hobby. Transitioning this species onto prepared foods generally requires significant patience — vitamin-enriched live brine shrimp and live or fresh clam, squid, and mysis are reported starting points; dried/frozen foods can sometimes be introduced gradually thereafter.

This species has been known to form monogamous pairs in the wild and may be kept as a male-female pair in aquaria; members of the same sex have been known to quarrel. It is not considered reef-safe and has been documented nipping stony corals, gorgonian polyps, zoantharians, and other sessile invertebrates.

A mature, well-established aquarium with live rock and natural algae growth is generally recommended before introduction. The species tends to spend time foraging across all swim zones.
breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding has been documented for this species. In the wild, it is oviparous and forms monogamous pairs. Successful aquarium reproduction is not considered achievable under typical hobby conditions.

additional.notes: Additional data point for tank volume (300 L); adult size listed as 7 cm appears to be an error or refers to a different metric — disregarded in favor of the 13–15 cm consensus from FishBase, LiveAquaria, and AquaInfo. Care rating 'delicate' consistent with advanced care classification.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented for this species. In the wild, the Four Eyed Butterflyfish is oviparous and forms monogamous pairs. Captive reproduction is not considered achievable under typical hobby conditions.
